Good morning or good afternoon depending on where you are guys. Thank you for joining us today and today we are with Brandy Sweezy. I want to tell you a little bit about Brandy. Brandy is probably one of my favourite people in the world and the person I'm most excited about interviewing because she is just an amazing person but full of energy and really happy to help anybody that crosses a path. But Brandy is unique because I heard about Brandy a long time before I spoke to her but I couldn't get to her because she is almost underground and she is so busy that it's almost impossible to hire her as well. And some of the top people in marketing and business in the world today are, you know, she's one of the most sought after people. She first came to prominence with Google Plus and Google Hangouts and you have a course that is called, I'm just going to go to that website, it's hangoutsforbusiness.com as well. And why we were interested in Brandy originally was we sort of figured out that the quality of the people who were coming to us from Google Hangouts was very good. You know, they converted better, they bought more stuff and it was a kind of inside secret almost. And as if that wasn't enough to become the sort of world leader in Google Hangouts, she then went on to YouTube and that was probably the number one person for anything to do with YouTube. And in fact, she's so cutting edge, something happened last night on YouTube that probably no one else in the world knows about Brandy. I mean, that's how close to what's happening that Brandy is. So hello, Brandy. Hi. So now my face matches the painting, the very bright painting in the background. So thank you for that beautiful introduction. I was like so humbled and honored to be here. So thank you guys. I appreciate that. So how did you, I mean, you had a career before you got online. How did you get to this? How did you get to doing this as a living? Yeah, it was pretty, it was crazy. Like I jokingly say, I really, and I do, I feel like I tripped into it. A lot of my mentors say, stop saying that, you know, it's preparation meets opportunities, what create this luck or whatever. But it was one of those things where I had, I've been in strategic developments, branding, public relations for, you know, on and off for 20 years. I'd stepped away from all of that, burnt out and then decided to become a public speaker and I went out and did the circuit with that and also got burnt out on being a road warrior and kind of got jaded by the industry. And then I went to a cabin in the woods for six months, waiting for some big lightning bolt to tell me what I was supposed to do. And the only lightning bolt was that happened was to not live in that particular town in that particular state. And I to this day, won't even fly through that state. So like my best friend called me up. She's like, listen, I love you. I'll support anything that you do, but you're perishing there. So I left there and I just came across some online courses and I didn't, I knew enough about branding. So I did a, you know, big deal about branding my name and the word mark and the colors and the photos and all of this big, huge influx. And then people would write me on Facebook, be like, I love your work. And I'm like, what work does that that you like to pay me for? You know, because that didn't even happen. You think to operate that way? It does have this big, you know, like it's just a WordPress site. But you know what, it just looked really good. It didn't look like I was bootstrapping it. And then I was kind of being a wimp about launching another brand and I worked really well under pressure. And so I said, that's it. I'm going to do this Google hangout thing on my birthday and I'll invite some of my, you know, friends I know will have a real raw conversation about struggling with passion and purpose and all of this stuff. And I did it on my birthday. I posted on my purse. I don't even think I had a fan page or business page at that point. And I posted it on my personal wall and 600 people showed up and I only posted 24 hours. So I looked around everybody's like, why are we all doing this? I'm like, everybody should be doing hangouts. Why isn't everybody doing hangouts? And so then everybody complained about them, you know, especially internet marketers, you know, we're like, well, it's not go to webinar. You can't do this. You can't do that. I'm like, I can't do that hard. So I, I paid a couple of experts myself because, you know, everything that could go wrong definitely did go wrong in that two hour show. Like I had my friends and my boyfriend at the time were all lined up on laptops, passing me posted notes with questions. I found out later on that actually nobody in fact had asked a question. They were just making things up to pass to me. So I didn't look stupid. But yeah, after that, I hired a couple of experts and I said, you know, and they were brilliant and I still adore them. They were very smart in the techie side of things, but they still were making it really overly difficult. So I was like, all right, well, I'll just figure out how to work this. And so I figured out how to work hangouts. I was like, okay, this is not that hard. And then people started offering me money. And I was like, uh, you know, it's free, right? Too late. You know, like hangouts are free. Uh, you could just go and do that. And it took me a while to get to the confidence level to be able to, um, understand my unique value to it. I think it was strategic development and marketing and, and different things. And, and to this day, I can say, um, you know, a lot more confidently, um, not even a lot more, like I definitely feel like I'm, I'm the person that knows them the best in how to use them in marketing, um, and how they benefit you in that way. So it was one of those things that people started offering me money. And I just kept on, you know, you have to go through this different confidence leaps. So I started off charging like $197 for consulting, which is a product price. Um, you know, then I just kept on making leaps. And then when I finally got to the price that I, that I charged today, the first time I said it, I kind of threw up in my mouth. Um, as I said it, it's not, there's no way this person's going to go for it. And they wrote the check and I was like, well, that's it. That's what I charged now. And, uh, so it was just one of those, you know, just, you know, I think it's just fast action, you know, I just was like, okay, I'm just going to keep on doing this. And I quite honestly was afraid to be limited by it because I felt like I was so much more than hangouts. Um, and then a few people said like leverage, you know, that you have a niche and that you're able to move forward in this niche. And so, you know, I, then I became much more accepting of it. And then I obviously have a course and, you know, I've gone on the train people to do what I've done. And, you know, and I love them. They're my favorite tribe. If you guys are, you know, you can't get ahold of me. I can refer you to one of them. They're fantastic. So once you just find a simpler way to do it, there's some simpler things to do. The biggest thing that's still missing and baffles me is that in order to be really successful in all of marketing, you have to understand the foundation, the infrastructure and really get the psychology of what the behavior is when people go to a certain platform. So you can't take what you do on Facebook and just transfer it to YouTube. And that's what a lot of the bigger brands that have embraced it early on. That was what they would do is they would take their big, huge advertising dollars and they would try to, you know, do either Hangouts or YouTube advertising or do a YouTube channel. And they were just taking it from one platform to picking one effort would go across all platforms and you really need to understand the language. So I'll talk a little bit about Hangouts first, because this was one of my first big epiphanies was that if you look at Hangouts, you know, blogging was what brought about transparency. We really felt like we were getting to know people with the, with the blogging thing, right? And then we had the tele seminars and so then we had some tonality with people. So we really were starting to get closer to people and we really are in the, the time in the last, you know, a few years, 10 years, you know, where we really want to brands are very much personal. They're not, you know, these big brands and this big wide gap between you and the people that you're trying to serve. And then we had the live webinars, obviously, which is what we're doing right now. So then you have like a go to webinars that I get to see some images. I get to see, you know, this person. I feel like I'm a little bit more connected to them. And then we got into YouTube advertising, which again, I think it's kind of funny that's everybody thinks this is the year of YouTube because really Gary Vaynerchuk is an amazing example of taking video in the Wine Library TV and now he owns Vayner Media. But he's been neglecting his YouTube channel for several years now and then he just launched again at the beginning of this year with the Ask Gary V show. Another great example is Marie Forleo, who, you know, just did a weekly, you know, it's called, you know, Q&A Tuesday. If you go back and go back through her YouTube history and look where she started, she was sitting at her dining room table. It took her a long time to get up to this level of these massive productions, you know, but then she ultimately ended up on Oprah and, you know, it's gone on to do, you know, crazy, you know, millions of dollar launches. And then it looked like Ryan Dice was going to give it a shot too with the Dice advice, you know, but same thing with all of this. What the secret to Marie's success was that every single Tuesday without fail, but the very first year that she did YouTube, she almost gave up because it takes a little bit of time to, you know, draw that audience. And the way that you're going to do that is obviously consistency. And then what happened was, you know, like a Marie or Gary Vee or even a Ryan Dice is that they have these big, huge production teams and people, you know, don't really remember where they came from. So what happens with that is you're starting to widen the empathy gap. Now, obviously we know when we're persuading or, you know, we're trying to make sales or we're trying to change someone's life and help them to do all the great things that they can do. We want to close that empathy gap. We want them to understand that, you know, we're like them. We've been there, done that. And that's not to, you know, to be manipulative in any way, but it just makes you more relatable when you're trying to help someone that you're like, well, if I look at a Marie, well, that's Marie, you know, she looks like Sex in the City and she has this big, huge studio and I could never be like her. And that's what I felt in the beginning, but I went back and looked through our old YouTube videos and she was sitting at a dining room table on her laptop and she just kept plugging away. So same thing when you're looking at YouTube and as we go into YouTube advertising, that's the other mistake that people are making. So you really have to get the foundational psychology of YouTube before you do anything. Right. So people want to do these big, huge, like, um, you know, everybody's into the live streams right now, like the kind of infomercially kind of ones you've seen them, we, they're, our friends have all done them. Um, you know, you're not, they're great, but then it's also putting it on another person that wants to do marketing that aspires to be like you. Um, it's putting the pressure on them to build out these big, huge studios and these big, huge shows. So let's also think when you think about YouTube or think about a TV show, you watch TV, the average 30 minute TV show is actually only 17 minutes of content. And our TV show is like 40 to 44 minutes of content. So to try to come out of the gate with a 30 minute show or an hour show or a five hour marathon show, I, I won't, I don't watch anything for five hours, except for when I'm like laying in bed in my cage, then I'll go on, I'll go on a, on a binge, but you know what I mean? But for the most part, if I see a YouTube video that's over 10 minutes, I'm like, peace out. Like, I don't like, you know, 10,000 other tabs that I have open that I'll never look at. Um, so it's really important. So when I tell people to start doing hangouts, start off with your laptop. Don't invest in anything until you start doing it consistently. And I think that everyone should just start off with like little five minute coffee talks, you know, like do five to seven minutes of whatever's flipping your fancy at the moment, you know? And just get good at, you know, like you'll get better on camera. And who cares what you look like on camera? Nobody comes out of the gate being great on camera. Then, okay. So now you've done, let's say five, then invest in like the webcam that I have, which is 70 bucks. You get it on Amazon. Um, it's called the Logitech C920. Um, you can get it on a little tripod, you know, being a woman, I like camera angles to be a certain way. Um, so, you know, you get that, you get that little $70 Logitech camera for the first six months I did hangouts. I didn't have that. Um, and I used my iPhone headset. Um, and just start getting consistent about it. And the beauty of the more you become consistent at that, then you'll start to get your calls to action better. You'll, it'll be more authentic. It won't be so tight and so rehearsed because remember the psychology of YouTube is I want to have a conversation with you. The other huge thing that everyone misses, even our biggest players and even my clients, somehow this won't register for them. YouTube is a search engine. People go there in pain. How do I install a WordPress blog? How do I write copy? How do I close a deal? How do I get clients? All these people title their, their hangouts, you know, brandy, Sweezy, uh, Halbert's and Mark talk about what like nobody cares about us. You know, what, what problem are we solving for them? You know, so to title your, your hangouts in a way that solves a pain because somebody is going there looking for pain. You know, usually when we think of YouTube videos, we think of cute kitten videos or music videos or whatnot. No, we've all gone on there to say how to get confusion soft work. You know, how do I tag or how do I filter? Or how, you know, like, how do I get something done in less than five minutes without having to pay somebody to do it? And usually you're in such pain at that point that you're willing to listen to anybody on YouTube that knows more than you do. Um, the other beauty of hangouts is they outrank everything. So if we were to do the exact same contents and we did it in this big professional studio and we did the exact same pace, you know, minute for minute, word for word, everything, and did a hangout and uploaded a YouTube video. I mean, as with all things, there's no mastery without actually doing it. You can read about it. You can buy all the courses. You can talk about it all day long, but until you actually do it, you're not going to get any better at it. You know, I will say, okay, here's my big one camera pointer though, because I do have to coach a lot of my clients on camera. You know, especially women always worry about the camera adding 10 pounds. That's the least of your concerns. The camera sucks 50% of your energy. So it really does. So when people get on hangouts, they're like, Hey, so what's happening? So just kind of like chilling here and I'm going to talk. Like somebody's going to like just throw their laptop out. They can't talk. So you'll notice like, if you were to meet me in person, I would seem like I'm exactly the same, but I'm amped 50% of what I normally am. But anybody that meets me in person feels like they know me because I'm exact to them on exactly the same. There's no difference, but I actually am my energy up 50% to be on camera. So I energized myself up. It's not me fake. It's not, um, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's not, it's just I'm upping my energy because I know that the camera's going to suck 50% of my energy out. So in order to keep people engaged and, you know, wanting to listen, I have to, I don't have to, you don't talk as fast as I do. I, I naturally talk this fast, but at least be aware of upping your energy, you know, lighting, makeup, sound, blah, blah, just practice upping your energy, getting your, your opening down, you know, practice getting your clothes down. Um, again, I love Marie Forleo. If you go back and watch like all of her old videos, it took her a long time to get her pace and now she's got her clothes perfect. And a lot of people ask me that, you know, like, so webinars are about, you know, like Tuesdays and Thursdays at whatever time. Um, to me, you know, I don't think there's any hard and fast rule. Know your audience. You know what I mean? Like, so you can go into YouTube and start looking at, you know, when people are engaging with you. I also tell people this too, because, you know, all of our egos get bruised. We like, Oh my God, I'm going to do a hangout. I'm so invested in it. I'm emotionally invested. Oh, one person showed up. I suck. My life sucks. I'm never doing this again. Um, but I can assure you 600 people showed up to my very first hangout and you never want that to happen. You don't want 600 people on your first hangout. You know, my slides didn't work. It was all kinds of jacked up. Um, you know, so don't even, don't have any kind of emotional attachment to an outcome, just get good at consistently doing it. Right. And then you'll, then when you get better, you'll start promoting it. Right. You'll start saying, yeah, I'm about to jump on a hangout. Uh, if you ever have a combination of consistency and random mixed up. It's the way that we would in an email auto response. Yeah. I mean, well gosh, so transparently again, I don't remember the last time I actually personally did a hangout. I do them with clients and stuff. So, uh, yeah, don't go look at my YouTube channel and use me as an example at all. Cause I don't remember the last time I did one. Um, you know, so yeah, so shame on me for being a consultant and not doing what I, uh, preach, but yeah. So what my goal was, I'll tell you when I first got into this was I was going to launch a show and it was a hashtag, J F B Y O U and it was just F and B U. Um, and I was going to invite people on to that were transparent in their business and in life and things like that. And so I was going to do it, you know, two to three times a week, always at the same time, no longer than 12 minutes. So it'd be like rapid fire, no longer than 12 minutes. Um, and then I just got too busy and I never got to launch that show. It's still one of my passion projects and someday I will actually launch it. Um, that was how I was going to do it. Uh, I'll also give this to you too. So my approach to hangouts. So you guys got the YouTube psychology of it. I also, um, if you're going to use it as a webinar replacement, this is another kind of, you know, uh, mistake that I see a lot of people make is that they become too slide dependent. Um, so they really want it to be a go to webinar. It's not going to go to hang out. It's, you know, it's called Google hangouts and there's go to webinar. Each of them speak their own language. I'm going to talk about this in a second, but, um, so understanding the language. So when all of the, um, the online markers got into hangouts, they wanted to do slides. They didn't want to be on camera. They'd get agitated about all these different things. No, you want to have a more natural conversation. And when you close, it's more of, um, so we know the webinar formula, right? You know, like, hi, this is who I am. This is why I'm so great. Here's the content now by my shit. Um, whereas hangouts are more of let's have a conversation. I'm sharing this content. Oh, by the way, here's a by myself, right? And I say pop up four, five max visuals if you're trying to pitch on a hangout. Um, and the reason being is because we kind of become, um, anesthetized to slides. We know the formula we've seen enough, um, where you want to, and I'm not saying give up, go to webinar. If you've got something that's working, making money, then by all means keep doing that, but you might want to add this in because again, we don't ever want to rely on just any one thing to make us money. Um, but on, on a hangout to be more like, you know, hey, I'm having a conversation. Be sure to post your questions. Yada, yada. Oh, here's a visual to support that. Okay. So blah, blah, blah. We're going to chat a little bit more. Oh, here's a visual support that. So you're training them to grab their pen every time you throw up a visual because it's a value visual. So I got to grab my pen. Right. So then when you go to make the offer, obviously that's going to be a visual. They got to grab their pen, right? Cause you've already trained them to grab their pen throughout the time. Um, and that's just some of the psychological stuff. Another piece of it is, is that I'm a firm believer in. Um, I say to always mention your keywords, like four times, not in a douchy way, but four times during, um, you know, your YouTube video or in your hangout. So you'll notice that I talk super fast. And then all my keywords are like, and I'll be talking about hangouts for business. And then I go out, I'll pick back up the pace to make sure, cause it like Siri doesn't understand a thing I say. So there's no way you can understand a thing I say, right? Now, if you, if you have the resources, then I tell everybody, get your, your, um, videos transcribed. So those hangouts that we're talking about is five to seven minute hangouts. I do those hangouts, get them transcribed, but the transcription and the closed captioning turned that into blog posts with the video. Right. And then I would be going back and forth between the two because, you know, we all have different ways of, you know, kinesthetic learning, right? There's some of us like to watch videos. Some of us want to download to be able to read. Some of us just want to hear it. We also, the more you can hit all of those, the, the you go gangbusters, you know? So sometimes people hear it and it seems a little labor intensive. For me, I'm a verbal processor, obviously. Um, so it's easier for me just to, to blather and then have someone transcribe it. So it has to be to write a blog post. I, I, I would rather bleed out here right now. Um, yeah, I mean, like, just not in writing is not my thing. Um, so it's easier for, you know, so like my assistant will be like, okay, just brain dump and then she'll take it and put it into something and then makes it an article or a download or whatever. Um, because she knows how I think. So she just makes me do a voice memo to her and then I blather and she finds the hot things and make something out of it. Um, so depending on your style. So if you'd like to write, so obviously this person, you know, does blog posts, then you just do like a two or three minute thing, just about what you're going to cover in the blog post and almost set it up like the podcasters page. Like, and so, you know, each iteration of the course had to be expanded upon on like what the sequence of events were, why the sequence was there, you know, you know, to found out that in the end, the course was 90% strategy and 10% what buttons to push. And I've discovered the same thing with YouTube advertising, you know, I have some of the biggest clients in the world. Um, and I, I spent 98% of my time explaining and, you know, 2% of my time actually plugging stuff in and watching it go. Um, so, you know, like never assume that people know stuff. Yeah, so take a picture of your business, blah, blah. Here's the big thing. So YouTube charges on a cost per view. Now, most of the time at CBC, CPA, CPM, all of these acronyms that, you know, they throw around YouTube is the only one that charges on a cost per view. You do not get charged if the person leaves the video before 30 seconds. So you could have 60,000 impressions, but only, you know, 600 views. So the end of the video or 30 seconds. I saw some expert out there saying, or no, I read an article last night and said, oh, yeah, so you want your videos to be around 20 seconds. Why would we want to do that? Then I'm getting charged for every single one. So you always have to be aware of where you're getting your information from. Like it was this great, you know, article. And then I got to that point, I was like, closed. So this is why it becomes a lot more manageable, but there is a secret sauce to how you make that happen. So when I work with clients, I actually personally script their videos. Sometimes we'll go into studio with them because it is so concise because I want one of two things to happen before 20 seconds. I only wanted to get to 30 seconds. I want one of two things to happen. I either want them to stick the ad. Right. Or I want them to be on my landing page. One of the two things before 20 seconds is actually 17 seconds. I don't know where I came up with that number, but all of the videos are 17. So like that's the call to actions. Then we want them off the video and on our landing page or we want them to skip that ad. Then we come back on at 32 seconds and we do another value proposition. And the reason being is going to be charged anyway. So I may as well give it another shot. Right. So most of the videos that I do are about 40 seconds. So it is five seconds. Wow. CTA by 17 seconds. Well, then click here. It's pretty much that quickly. Um, and then at 32 seconds, he comes. So he fades out. The call to action stays there. He fades out. So you have no choice. Skip ad or click here. One of the two things for 17 seconds until 32 seconds. Those are your only two choices. Nothing's happening. Nothing. These two things. And then 32 seconds, he comes back on and says, well, in this, you know, my lead magnet, I share with you, yada, yada, yada. Cause now we have to pay for him anyway. Yeah. You, you, you want to, so here, here's the big thing is that you need to have a Google compliant site. Obviously they are very much driven by their end user experience. Right. I mean, that's their whole thing. That's what the Google slap was about. That's, you know, um, you can get ads disapproved. I've had ads disapproved for, for silly stuff. And then, you know, and then it wasn't so silly because then I realized I missed something. Um, so they do look at the website. You can drive straight to a landing page. The biggest thing that you need to have before you start doing this is a well-seasoned site. So you need to have a, um, you know, a main domain, you know, like how pathology, you know, dot com on there, there were, you couldn't just have a, um, there'd have to be blog posts and contact and privacy and FTC and all of that kind of stuff on there. And there has to be some kind of way of attaching yourself to it, even though it's just a little logo with a link. Okay. Yeah. So like, so, so, you know, like on the one client that I was just talking about where we do the fade. So we have a really seasoned site. Um, so we, when it's seasoned is only seven, eight blog posts, um, contact information, you know, um, you know, like all the norm, privacy, earnings disclaimer. You, you make any kind of claims, even if it is, uh, I had one client that was, uh, promised to help women find conscious men to date. Um, but because there was a claim, uh, we had to have a little, um, it wasn't just a disclaimer, it couldn't be hyperlinked out. It didn't have to be, but I, you know, I told my ad words record, her only value was to, to become best friends of the clients department so that I know these things in advance. Cause I don't mind pushing the envelope a little bit, but obviously I want to stay happy with Google, uh, that if you just had like a little sentence with a little asterisk that that was enough and then you could hyperlink out the, the, the long I'm making a claim thing. So yeah, all clients have to have a, um, I call it a grown up business looking site, you know, like seven, eight blog posts where you look like a real business. There's contact information on there. Ideally there's a phone number. Um, that doesn't have to be obvious, but, uh, it's just a Google compliance site. And then, uh, you can't do like to, uh, to a lead pages or to a click funnels or whatever it needs to be hosted on your domain because there's display URL and there's a destination URL and they have to match in some way. Um, so you can't have it be like a, you know, a lead pages.com forward slash, you know, whatever you need to go ahead and host, uh, lead pages on your server. Um, you can still use lead pages. I mean, that's, you know, a quick and easy way. Click funnels is a quick and easy way. Um, they're all working on it. I don't know if they've found a work around yet or not, but for me, like, you know, the level of my clients, they, they all are required to make a seasoned main domain with blog post on it. All of those things about contact, you know, you know, some navigation in it and just a logo and then we take that logo and then we put it on a landing page. And so we just have that little, like the logo is like this big. So here's the biggest thing is that you really have to understand the language. First of all, right. So that's what we're talking about is, you know, a lot of people again, we'll take their, you know, they'll take their YouTube ads and try to put on Facebook or the Facebook ads and try to put on YouTube or Instagram or Twitter or whatever. Every single platform speaks a completely different language and they're completely motivated by different things. You know, so, you know, the person that I see crushing it is Gary, Gary Vaynerchuk, Gary V. Right. If you'll notice, he'll take, he'll take one video, but it's, it's edited to a way for YouTube. It's edited to a way for Instagram. It's edited to a way for Twitter and it's different on Facebook. It's the same, but different, right? So you have to learn the languages of the platforms before you even get started. So when someone comes to me, he says, oh, this is my whole here, learning different languages. I, you know, really, you have to understand the language before you ever even think of it getting started. And so I have clients come to me and say, oh, well, my affiliate funnel converts to 60%. Well, that's, hey, that's called endorsement traffic. Oh, well, my Facebook funnel converts that. Yeah. Okay. That's great. That's called curiosity traffic. And we're going to go through this really quickly now. So this are the things that my clients have to, have to sign. Understanding traffic, traffic risk, the 28 page contracts, understanding a funnel and then compliance. And the biggest one being understanding traffic. And this is again created out of my pain. So the big one, especially in the internet marketing spaces, everybody likes to talk about is affiliate traffic, right? That's endorsement traffic. That's where, you know, a bunch of friends are gathered around you and they're saying, hey, this person's really cool. This is really awesome. This is going to be great for you. Right. That's obviously going to be a really high converting traffic because you've been, you know, been endorsed by someone that they should trust you and move on. The next one is curiosity traffic. Now, so Google itself is curiosity traffic. I don't know if the Facebook ad guys, while I'll describe it, and if they disagree, then that's fine too. But I think Facebook is curiosity traffic as well. So I'm in Facebook and just kind of cruising and I'm on a social network, right? Because Facebook isn't where I go to solve problems. It's where I go to stalk people or, you know, to talk about how wonderful life is or whatnot. So, but then I see something like, oh, that looks interesting. So that's curiosity traffic, right? Google, when somebody goes into Google and says, how do I solve this? And all of those ads come up, right? They're kind of, they're kind of in pain, but they're still curious, right? They're like, oh, okay, that one looks a little more interesting. That headline looks a little more interesting. That compels me to go there instead of to this search result, right? So still curious, they're curious. Now, this YouTube traffic is ice cold, interruption traffic. They are on a certain desire path and you just fucked with their day, right? So you have to understand the, how you're going to talk to them because they are going there to solve a problem and you just mess with their day or they're going there to watch a Q kit video and you just mess with the day. Now, there's nothing more empowering, especially for all of us that grew up with, you know, commercials our entire lives, you know, to be able to push, skip that. So everyone is just hovering, just say my name, just say my name. I'm gonna skip this ad. You know what I mean? Like they are so empowered. So you have to understand that mindset going into this. So totally different funnel, total different psychology, total different report builds, all of that. Now it's insanely effective if you understand this, but if you don't understand this, which, you know, say well over 90% of the ads that I see definitely don't understand it. And I watch a ton of ads, you know, don't they still don't understand it. So, you know, those big, huge movie trailers, it's like, I'm just waiting to skip. You know, I mean, now not so much because I'll watch them because it's my business, but and this is such a great, you get a chance to watch this YouTube video. It's hilarious. But it's this panda just walks up and knocks this person's, you know, card over, right? Like, you know, the guy's trying to, in the grocery store, his kids driving nuts. He just wants to get more food. He's hungry, he wants to go home, his wife's probably mad at him. And this panda just came over and knocked this card over. So when you do YouTube advertising, you're well aware of this is what's going through their mind. So it's even more important to really laser target. And I'm going to talk about how to do this. And this is what makes YouTube so brilliant is what I was talking about is being able to put the ad in front of people that you know are interested in this pain point. And the beauty of it is they're in pain. Right. So I wouldn't go and put my ads for, you know, my hangouts course or my YouTube ad course on, you know, Gwen Stefani video or on a, you know, whoever the tube stars are, like that would make no sense to me. They're not my demographic. They would not like they would despise me, right? They're not my age group. They're not they're not entrepreneurs. They're not, you know, all of these different things. So it would be foolish to just throw money at that. And then the thing is, and I'll use it because I live in Vegas now, with that, the house always wins, right? AdWords is always going to win. You know, I mean, like, I shouldn't say always, but, you know, it's definitely weighted towards them when you leave it up to them to spend your money. So getting really crystal clear on who that person is and what kind of pain they are in, and being able to solve that in advance of the video that they're about to watch. Does that make sense? Uh-oh. Did I lose you guys? Yeah. Nope. Okay. All right. Yeah. Nope. So I've been using this a lot lately. I used to always use the obvious dating analogy. It's like, you know, it's always a numbers game. So if you just walked up to, you know, a thousand girls and said, hey, you want to come home with me? Eventually, you know, maybe somebody would by three or four o'clock in the morning. But if you walked up and said, hey, you want to go have coffee, what not? But my new one is because that's not so PC. Thank God this group's not so PC. So it's all right. But I like the food analogy too. And the big thing that's happened with funnels over the last couple of years is they've gotten, you know, tripwires and, you know, lead magnets and all these things, people thinking that first lead magnet needs to be a 50-page e-book. Well, we want to move them through a funnel and we want to keep on giving them a little taste, right? We want to keep on getting to know them and we want to show that we have something to give them. And we just, but we only want to give them a morsel so that they'll want more, right? So they start trusting us in the building report throughout. There's little things like on your landing page instead of say, never say spam, say, you know, we respect your privacy here. You know, definitely always let them know what's coming next. A big mistake I see, particularly like somebody coming off of a really expensive affiliate funnel, they're used to being able to go lead magnet, you know, wow, VSL Biotin, right? That's, you can try that, but it would be better if you said, hey, so here when you enter email, we're going to email you this, you know, your freebie. Just to let you know on the next page, I'm going to share with you in this video. Also video to video converts better. And again, remembering the language of the audience, they're YouTube, they've gone to YouTube to watch videos to send them just to a static page, you know, is not really, you know, ideal. I mean, it'll still convert, but a better would be to, you know, you get them off of YouTube, you get them to your landing page and you have a video there, you know, 90 seconds just saying, hey, thanks so much for showing up. Just to let you know, as promised, right here is my free league medicaid. Once you enter your email address, I'm going to go ahead and email that to you. Just to give you a heads up, there's a video on the next page. If you want to turn down your sound, look forward to answering any questions, blah, blah, right? So you look, so this guy, like the pan, just messed with his day. Well, what most people do is go ahead and try to take them right from the guy's in a grocery store and wanted to buy 50 bucks worth of food to a thousand dollar caviar frittata, right? Well, that's a huge stretch, right? So what you want to do is more take them through a little tasting menu. What if I said, hey, just get your attention, I have a better solution for you, here's just a little taste. When people do these big, huge front ends of their funnels, they overwhelm people, right? We're definitely in the ADHD era where we're like, oh my God, and now I have an ebook, now I have your tripwire, my God, that's like four hours worth of work, and now you want me to buy something worth 500 to a thousand dollars? Well, let me finish these two first and then see if I'll even open that first one first. Instead, you only want to keep on giving them just a little bit more to get them used to you and keep on feeding them just a little bit more and a little bit more because if somebody just showed up and gave you this big, you know, monster, I love this photograph, like, where somebody just stopped you on the street and then said, hey, here, come and pick all of these, right? And you weren't even hungry to begin with, you know, then it's kind of, you know, silly. So you want to keep on building those little small pieces of rapport and really look at your funnel and what you're offering them and that you just keep on building and kind of same thing that, you know, you guys have been at this a long time, just same thing, you're building on the foundation, we're adding to it, building on the foundation, adding to it, right? You want to keep doing that and not just, you know, I think in the last few years, everybody's been trained to, you know, give all of their best stuff right out of the gate, you know, and do the lead back in the trip, just give them everything and then they're going to buy your core offer. Well, whereas you could just keep on saying, okay, so now that you got this, see, see how easy that was, you got that done now, this, oh, see how easy that was now, oh, see, see how that, you know, and this isn't something new that I'm talking about, it's just going back to what works instead of doing all of these really big things and overwhelming.
